stem: You are a clinical pharmacist in an inpatient setting asked to develop a transitions of care (TOC) pharmacy service at your institution. Which task is most appropriate to suggest for an inpatient TOC pharmacist pilot?
choices: Perform weekly virtual visits with recently discharged patients
Provide medication therapy management (MTM) services to patients at high risk of readmission
Conduct medication reconciliations at discharge for selected patients
Perform discharge telephone calls 24 to 48 hours for patients at high risk of readmission
exp: Pharmacists in all settings can positively influence effective TOC. Community and ambulatory clinic pharmacists are authorized and best positioned to perform MTM services and comprehensive medication reviews and conduct virtual visits with patients as deemed appropriate under clinical agreements (Answers A and B are incorrect). Inpatient pharmacists are encouraged to conduct medication reconciliations at all TOC in the inpatient setting to improve patient outcomes, not just at discharge (Answer C is incorrect). Inpatient pharmacists with established TOC services make significant impacts by con

stem: A 21-year-old woman presents to the clinic with genital itching and vesicles on her vulva. She is sexually active with one partner who has a history of herpes. Her partner does not always use a condom when they have sex. She was appropriately treated for her initial herpes simplex infection 10 months ago. This is her seventh recurrence since initial diagnosis. Which therapy is best to recommend?
choices: Valacyclovir 500 mg orally twice daily to be used for 5 days whenever she notices a recurrence beginning
Acyclovir 400 mg orally three times daily to be used for 10 days whenever she notices a recurrence beginning
Suppressive therapy with famciclovir 250 mg orally three times daily
Suppressive therapy with valacyclovir 500 mg orally daily
exp: Patient-initiated therapy is important for people with occasional recurrences (fewer than six per year) of herpes simplex virus infection because recurrent infections resolve more rapidly than the initial infection. Antiviral agents should be initiated as soon as possible. Because the patient is having several recurrences (six or more a year), patient-initiated therapy is insufficient (Answers A and B are incorrect). Suppressive therapy is given twice daily or once daily, not three times daily; therefore, suppressive therapy with famciclovir 250 mg orally three times daily is inappropriate (An

stem: A 21-year-old with new-onset schizophrenia presents to your office for a medication check. They were hospitalized for a psychotic episode 6 months ago, for which they received quetiapine. They currently take quetiapine 600 mg at bedtime. They have gained 11.34 kg (current weight 86.18 kg, current BMI 32.6 kg/m2). They are alert and oriented × 3, neatly groomed, and dressed appropriately. They continue to hear voices that make negative comments about them. The voices still make them feel sad, but they no longer feel compelled to do anything about it. They believe the voices are real. They remain socially withdrawn and live at home with their parents. Which is the best recommendation?
choices: Continue quetiapine at current dose
Change quetiapine to cariprazine
Change quetiapine to clozapine
Change quetiapine to olanzapine
exp: This patient's response to quetiapine has plateaued, and it is the first agent this patient has tried for schizophrenia. This patient has also gained 13% of their body weight on quetiapine. The FDA recommends considering a change in antipsychotic if a patient gains 7% or more of their body weight while taking an antipsychotic. Therefore, the risks of continuing this medication outweigh the benefits (Answer A is incorrect). Olanzapine, together with clozapine, is associated with the greatest degree of weight gain, so it may not be a good choice in this patient (Answer D is incorrect). This pati

stem: A hospital wishes to develop a new alert to use in their electronic health record (EHR) with inpatients, emergency department visits, and clinic visits to increase immunization rates. The plan is to alert providers of recommended immunizations according to information in the EHR so that patients and caregivers can be given information, ask questions, and be invited to receive these immunizations. Success will be measured by monthly tracking of immunizations and visits/admissions for related infections. Which is most likely to occur over the first year if a successful program is implemented?
choices: Increased reimbursement for hospital admissions
Improved health literacy regarding immunizations in patients and caregivers
Higher state ranking for immunization administration rates
Improved immunization rates in the population served by the hospital
exp: When EHRs are implemented, additional technology can add a clinical decision support system to help prescribers identify needed interventions, such as immunizations. Advice can be tailored to age, sex, time of year, time since previous doses, and other parameters found in the EHR. Therefore, clinical decision support can be used to improve immunization rates, but only for individuals served by the hospital (Answer D is correct). Higher state ranking would take longer than 1 year to realize based on the actions of one hospital (Answer C is incorrect). Hospital admission reimbursements would not

stem: A 49-year-old man presents to the primary care clinic with concerns of ongoing knee pain for the past 8 months. Radiography of the right knee reveals no degenerative changes. No traumatic events are reported, and physical examination findings are negative for injuries. His medical history is significant for recurrent bouts of gastroesophageal reflux and hypertension. Which is best to recommend for this patient?
choices: Topical capsaicin
Oral naproxen
Topical diclofenac
Oral celecoxib
exp: Although there are no pertinent radiographic findings, the patient's expression of pain should be believed. Radiographic findings, or the lack thereof, are not predictive of pain; therefore, he should be treated. Topical capsaicin has a conditional, not strong, recommendation for use, according to the treatment guidelines (Answer A is incorrect). The patient has a history of gastroesophageal reflux and hypertension. Although not of concern from a mortality/serious adverse effect perspective, the oral route can be avoided (Answers B and D are incorrect). Topical diclofenac has been shown to be

stem: A 34-year-old woman is 8 weeks into a third pregnancy. The patient has had significant nausea and vomiting throughout the first trimester of each of the prior pregnancies. She is currently experiencing nausea and vomiting that has not improved with nonpharmacologic options, such as the use of ginger products and dietary changes. Which recommendation is most appropriate at this time?
choices: Continue nonpharmacologic options
Initiate pyridoxine
Initiate doxylamine
Initiate ondansetron
exp: Because this patient's symptoms have not resolved with current nonpharmacologic approaches, pharmacologic treatment is warranted (Answer A is incorrect). Pyridoxine is an appropriate first-line therapy to treat nausea and vomiting of pregnancy and can be used with or without doxylamine (Answer B is correct). Doxylamine should not be used as monotherapy for nausea and vomiting of pregnancy (Answer C is incorrect). Ondansetron should only be considered if nausea and vomiting are not controlled with first-line agents, and a possible increased risk of cardiac birth defects must be considered (Answ

stem: A 34-year-old presents to your outpatient clinic to discuss smoking cessation. This patient is being considered for bariatric surgery and needs to be off all nicotine products for at least 3 months before surgery. The patient has a 20 pack-year history of smoking cigarettes (smokes 1 pack/day, first cigarette within 30 minutes of awakening). They have previously tried to quit "cold turkey." This patient is almost through their workup for surgery and does not want to wait longer than needed. Which treatment would be best?
choices: Bupropion
Nicotine gum
Nicotine patch
Varenicline
category: 2B6. Patient and Caregiver Education and Counseling
exp: Nicotine replacement products that are used as needed, such as nicotine gum, are not as successful at helping patients quit smoking (Answer B is incorrect). This patient needs to be off nicotine products for at least 3 months before surgery. Using a modality such as the nicotine patch may delay the patient's surgery date, because they have to wean off cigarettes and then wean off the patch (Answer C is incorrect). According to the ATS clinical guidelines, varenicline is recommended over both bupropion and the nicotine patch for smoking cessation (Answer D is correct; Answer A is incorrect).

stem: A 67-year-old woman with no significant medical history presents to her primary care physician with reports of stiffness and swelling of her hands over the past 3 weeks. On examination, redness and warmth are noted bilaterally at the proximal interphalangeal joints. Radiographic evaluation of her hand joints reveals joint space narrowing and bone erosion. Blood tests show positive rheumatoid factor. The patient is diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis (RA). In addition to methotrexate, which treatment recommendation is best to initiate at this time?
choices: Hydroxychloroquine
Upadacitinib
Etanercept
Prednisone
exp: Treatment for RA is recommended within the first 3 months of diagnosis. Nonbiologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) are the recommended first-line treatment, with methotrexate being the preferred agent. Because the patient is experiencing symptoms of inflammation, symptom control is recommended in addition to methotrexate (Answer D is correct). Treatment with multiple DMARDs is no longer recommended first line, and combination therapy should only be considered after methotrexate is optimized (Answers A, B, and C are incorrect).

stem: A 39-year-old woman who presents with a 4-day history of nasal drainage and nasal congestion reports a history of a scratchy throat and facial pressure. The nasal discharge was originally clear, but today, it became purulent. She has had no fever or cough, but her current symptoms seem to be worsening today. She takes montelukast, levothyroxine, and calcium carbonate with vitamin D. She has not seen a doctor in almost a year. Which best assesses this patient's presentation?
choices: The patient is having an asthma exacerbation and should be treated with inhaled beta agonists
The patient is having a reaction to seasonal allergens and should be treated with antihistamines
The patient has bacterial sinusitis and should be treated with antibiotics
The patient has viral sinusitis and should be treated symptomatically
exp: With no wheezing and shortness of breath, it is doubtful that this patient is having an asthma exacerbation (Answer A is incorrect). Since the patient is now having purulent nasal drainage, this is not seasonal allergies (Answer B is incorrect). Symptoms for bacterial sinusitis this early (4 days) would be much more severe (Answer C is incorrect). At this point, the patient most likely has viral sinusitis and should be treated symptomatically and monitored for bacterial sinusitis (persistent symptoms or worsening symptoms after improvement) (Answer D is correct).

stem: Given typical use, which method of contraception is most effective to prevent pregnancy?
choices: Combined oral contraceptive
Copper intrauterine device (IUD)
Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA) intramuscular injection
Progestin-only oral contraceptive
exp: The oral contraceptives (combined and progestin-only) have a typical use efficacy of 91% for pregnancy prevention (Answers A and D are incorrect). Implants and IUDs have a typical use efficacy of >99% (Answer B is correct). The DMPA intramuscular injection has a typical use efficacy of 94% (Answer C is incorrect).

stem: A 36-year-old is currently 34 weeks gestation with her first pregnancy. She has no significant past medical history. Her blood pressure at her appointment last week was 146/94 mm Hg, and at today's appointment is 152/96 mm Hg. Her urine dipstick at today's appointment shows 2+ protein. All other lab parameters are within normal limits. What treatment is best to recommend?
choices: Labor induction for immediate delivery
Start nifedipine extended-release 30 mg orally daily
Start magnesium sulfate 1 g intravenously every 8 hours
Start labetalol 100 mg intravenously every 12 hours
exp: This patient is experiencing pregnancy-induced hypertension because she is currently further along than 20 weeks gestation and does not have a pre-existing history of hypertension. She has preeclampsia without severe features (BP < 160/110 mm Hg with otherwise normal labs and no warning signs); therefore, expectant management with an antihypertensive is appropriate and immediate delivery is not necessary before 37 weeks (Answer A is incorrect). The use of oral nifedipine is a safe and appropriate choice for pregnancy-induced hypertension (Answer B is correct). A magnesium sulfate infusion should only be used to prevent progression to eclampsia in the

stem: A 71-year-old man (height 175 cm, weight 93 kg) has a medical history of atrial fibrillation (AF) (CHA2DS2-VASc score 2) with initial onset 3 years ago and bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement 4 months ago for management of symptomatic aortic regurgitation. He presents to the outpatient cardiology clinic requesting next steps for management of anticoagulation. Other conditions include hypertension and obesity. His medications include warfarin 5 mg orally daily, metoprolol succinate 100 mg orally daily, and losartan 100 mg orally daily. His INR is 2.6, and all other laboratory values are within normal limits. The patient is concerned about long-term warfarin management and is seeking alternatives. Which best describes the evidence-based approach to managing this patient's valvular heart disease with AF?
choices: Continue dose-adjusted warfarin to an INR target of 2.5
Discontinue warfarin and initiate aspirin 81 mg orally daily
Discontinue warfarin and initiate rivaroxaban 20 mg orally daily
Discontinue warfarin. No additional antithrombotic therapy is indicated
exp: Patients with a surgical aortic bioprosthetic valve with an indication for anticoagulation because of AF and a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 2 can discontinue warfarin after 3-6 months and start guideline-directed anticoagulation such as rivaroxaban 20 mg orally daily (Answer C is correct). Although warfarin may be a reasonable anticoagulant for the prevention of stroke in a patient with AF, the appropriate warfarin target for a bioprosthetic aortic valve is an INR of 2.5 (range 2.0-3.0); Answer A is inferior because the patient is seeking an alternative to warfarin, and a DOAC is acceptable for AF beyond 3 months after bioprosthetic valve placement (Answer A is incorrect). In a patient with a bioprosthetic aortic

stem: A patient with diabetes and G3b, A3 chronic kidney disease is currently taking lisinopril. She has a blood pressure of 118/78 mm Hg and presents complaining of a dry cough. She has the following stable laboratory values after trying appropriate diet modifications for the past 3 months: serum creatinine 2.7 mg/dL, serum potassium 4.6 mEq/L, serum calcium 9.5 mg/dL, serum phosphate 5.8 mg/dL, serum albumin 2.5 g/dL, serum parathyroid hormone 301 pg/mL. Which medications would be most appropriate to initiate at this time?
choices: Discontinue lisinopril, initiate losartan and calcium acetate
Continue lisinopril, initiate sevelamer carbonate
Continue lisinopril, initiate calcium acetate
Discontinue lisinopril, initiate losartan and sevelamer carbonate
exp: This patient has diabetes and chronic kidney disease (CKD) category G3b, A3 along with hyperphosphatemia and hyperparathyroidism. A corrected calcium calculation also reveals the presence of hypercalcemia [measured Ca + (0.8)(4 - serum albumin) = 9.5 mg/dL + (0.8)(4 - 2.5) = 9.5 + 1.2 = 10.7 mg/dL]. Because of the continually elevated phosphate level, initiation of a phosphate binder is appropriate. With the corrected calcium elevated, choosing a calcium-containing phosphate binder places the patient at greater risk of further hypercalcemia (Answers A and C are incorrect). Sevelamer carbonate

stem: A 59-year-old man with New York Heart Association class III heart failure with reduced ejection fraction presented to the ED yesterday with a 13.6-kg weight gain over the past month, bilateral lower extremity edema, and worsening shortness of breath on exertion. His home dose of furosemide is 80 mg orally twice daily. Pertinent laboratory values include Na 136 mEq/L, K 4.8 mEq/L, serum urea nitrogen 20 mg/dL, SCr 2.0 mg/dL (baseline 1.3 mg/dL), and N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide 1540 pg/mL. Today, his laboratory values remain unchanged. His blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg and pulse rate is 90 beats/min. His oxygen saturation is 89% on 6-L nasal cannula. The patient was initiated on furosemide 40 mg intravenously twice daily with an inadequate response to diuresis and is now experiencing shortness of breath at rest. Which next step is most appropriate to promote diuresis?
choices: Add hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg orally daily
Increase furosemide to 80 mg intravenously twice daily
Administer milrinone 0.125-mcg/kg/h continuous infusion
Administer intravenous nitroglycerin 5-µg/min continuous infusion
exp: After an ineffective initial attempt at diuresis, this patient requires escalated diuretic strategies to address his volume overload. Guidelines recommend initial intravenous loop diuretic 1 to 2.5 times the patient's home diuretic dose either as intermittent bolus or by continuous infusion, according to the DOSE trial (Answer B is correct). Optimization of high-dose intravenous loop diuretics is recommended before adding a thiazide or thiazide-like diuretic because of the increased risk of impaired renal function and hypokalemia seen in the CLOROTIC trial (Answer A is incorrect). Intravenous

stem: Between midnight and 2:00 a.m. on a Sunday morning, a tornado touched down in a city of 100,000, causing injury for 150 individuals, who were taken to the closest emergency department. Only two pharmacists and one pharmacy technician worked on this shift. The hospital's emergency plan did not include mechanisms to bring in additional pharmacy staff. The hospital pharmacy director was alerted, and after arrival to the department, she called for all available employees, including pharmacists and pharmacy technicians, to come to work in order to manage the patient load. Out of 50 pharmacists and 50 pharmacy technicians, only 20% of pharmacists and 40% of pharmacy technicians reported for duty within 2 hours. The hospital's board of directors agreed that this caused undesired outcomes in the treatment of several patients with injuries, and they wished to evaluate contributing factors to address to improve future responses if a similar disaster occurs. Which is the best tool to use?
choices: Root cause analysis
Routine emergency drills
Histogram
Driver diagram
exp: Root cause analysis is usually focused on an adverse outcome in an individual patient where an interprofessional team was responsible for the outcome, rather than a system-wide failure (Answer A is incorrect). If the pharmacy department had a plan to increase capacity during a surge, routine emergency drills would be useful; however, this pharmacy department needs to develop a plan before routine drills would be useful (Answer B is incorrect). The histogram tool displays continuous data over time, so it would not help identify ways to improve response to an emergency, which occurs sporadically

stem: A 47-year-old woman with a 7-year history of type 2 diabetes and a 3-year history of diabetic kidney disease is receiving insulin glargine 36 units once daily. She weighs 74 kg. Last week, her A1c was 7.6% (personal goal less than 7%). Her average morning, fasting glucose is 202 mg/dl. Her average postprandial glucose after breakfast, lunch, and dinner is 121 mg/dL, 110 mg/dL, and 130 mg/dL, respectively. Her most recent serum creatinine was 1.9 mg/dL (estimated glomerular filtration rate 24 mL/minute/1.73 m2). Which is best option to improve this patient's glycemic control?
choices: Increase insulin glargine
Add insulin aspart at breakfast
Add metformin
Add dapagliflozin
exp: This patient's postprandial glucose averages are good, whereas her fasting glucose average is very poor. Basal insulins, such as insulin glargine, help improve morning, fasting glucose concentrations, making it the correct answer (Answer A is correct). Adding a rapid-acting insulin is incorrect because it will not improve the patient's fasting blood glucose levels and may lead to postprandial hypoglycemia caused by already tight postprandial control (Answer B is incorrect). Metformin is contraindicated due to estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) of less than 30 mL/min/1.73m2 (Answer C i

stem: A pharmacist is discussing smoking cessation strategies with a patient and would like to use the process of "planning" through motivational interviewing (MI) to help the patient develop a quit-day strategy. Which best depicts the response that uses this foundational process of MI?A "To summarize what I've heard, you really want to quit smoking but think it would be difficult, given how long you've been smoking."B "You are worried the barriers for quitting will outweigh your benefits you told me."C "What are some of the main reasons you'd like to quit smoking?"D "Given what you've told me so far, what do you think you'll do next?"
choices: “To summarize what I’ve heard, you really want to quit smoking but think it would be difficult, given how long you’ve been smoking.”
“You are worried the barriers for quitting will outweigh your benefits you told me.”
“What are some of the main reasons you’d like to quit smoking?”
“Given what you’ve told me so far, what do you think you’ll do next?”
exp: The question in Answer D uses planning because it allows patients to create their own plan and promotes self-efficacy and autonomy (Answer D is correct). The statement in Answer A uses the process of focusing by strategically centering on both the provider's and the patient's goals but does not focus on the plan (Answer A is incorrect). Answer B is a simple reflective listening statement that would help establish trust and a relationship, but again, it would not focus on the plan (Answer B is incorrect). Finally, the question in Answer C may be useful to ask, but this is the "evoking" process

stem: A hospital brings in a new type of infusion pump to use in the outpatient infusion center. You are assigned to provide training to the nursing and pharmacy staff who will use the pump. The training you develop involves a 20-minute video showing the steps in using the pump and troubleshooting to solve specific problems. You wish to assess the effectiveness of the video as part of the educational activity. Which most accurately shows that participants are capable of using the pumps?
choices: A survey of participant perception of ability
An examination of each individual's performance in practice
A quiz to evaluate participants' knowledge at the end of the video
An anonymous questionnaire evaluating the video
exp: Observation or examination of performance in practice is the most accurate assessment of learning as the ultimate outcome of training (Answer B is correct). Participant perception of ability does not signify that learning occurred (Answer A is incorrect). A quiz to evaluate participants' knowledge would show whether participants gained knowledge in using the infusion pumps, though they may not have long-term retention of that knowledge (Answer C is incorrect). An anonymous questionnaire that evaluates the video would provide information focused on participant satisfaction with training but wou

stem: A 9-month-old child is brought by her father to the pediatrician after having visited 3 days earlier. She initially presented with fever, poor oral intake, irritability, and tugging on her left ear. Her diagnosis at that time was unilateral acute otitis media (AOM) without otorrhea. The pediatrician had elected to delay antibiotic prescribing; however, the patient has not had much improvement in her symptoms. Her allergies include a previous rash and hives to penicillin. Which would be the best recommendation for this patient?
choices: Administer cefdinir oral solution for 10 days
Administer amoxicillin (90 mg/kg/day of amoxicillin component) for 10 days
Continue to watch, and delay antibiotic prescribing for another 48 hours
Administer clindamycin oral solution for 10 days
exp: Delayed antibiotic prescribing is an acceptable option for children older than 6 months. For children between 6 months and 2 years of age, this approach is recommended only if the otitis media is unilateral, the symptoms are mild, or the diagnosis is uncertain. For patients in whom delayed antibiotic prescribing is initiated, an antibiotic should be prescribed if symptoms worsen or there is no improvement in 48-72 hours (Answer C is incorrect). Clindamycin with or without a third-generation cephalosporin may be considered in patients for whom first-line treatment regimens have failed; however,

stem: A 10-year-old boy with severe eosinophilic asthma is determined to be a candidate for add-on biologic therapy because of persisting symptoms with multiple exacerbations in the past year despite optimized therapy. The patient has no contraindications to biologic therapies with a baseline blood eosinophil count of 175 cells/mm3. Which biologic treatment is best to recommend?
choices: Intravenous reslizumab
Subcutaneous benralizumab
Subcutaneous mepolizumab
Subcutaneous omalizumab
exp: Mepolizumab is best because it is indicated for add-on maintenance therapy in severe asthma in patients 6 years and older with eosinophilic asthma (Answer C is correct). Reslizumab and benralizumab are incorrect; although both are indicated for eosinophilic asthma, they are not appropriate because of the patient's age. Reslizumab is indicated for patients 18 years and older, and benralizumab is indicated for patients 12 years and older (Answers A and B are incorrect). Omalizumab is an anti-immunoglobulin E agent specifically indicated for severe allergic asthma (Answer D is incorrect).

stem: A 70-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease and dysphagia requires medication administration by a G-tube. His current medication regimen includes metoprolol succinate 50 mg extended release once daily, clopidogrel 75 mg once daily, omeprazole 40-mg delayed-release capsule once daily, and duloxetine 30-mg delayed-release capsule once daily. Which best depicts the medication that can be safely crushed and administered by the G-tube?
choices: Metoprolol succinate
Clopidogrel
Omeprazole
Duloxetine
category: 2C2. Non-Formulary Drug Requests and Therapeutic Interchange
exp: Clopidogrel is a tablet that can be crushed without affecting its efficacy (Answer B is correct). Metoprolol succinate is extended release and should not be crushed (Answer A is incorrect). Duloxetine and omeprazole are both delayed-release capsules, so they should not be crushed but may be opened to be administered by G-tube (Answers C and D are incorrect).

stem: A 23-year-old man (weight 70 kg) with neurogenic bladder is admitted to the ICU for septic shock secondary to complicated UTI because of an infected indwelling urinary catheter. He has received an appropriate fluid bolus with balanced crystalloids and is currently receiving norepinephrine 0.1 µg/kg/min with a mean arterial pressure of 55 mm Hg. Records from prior hospitalizations show the presence of extended-spectrum β-lactamase-producing Klebsiella pneumoniae in the urine, and empiric meropenem 1 g intravenously every 8 hours is initiated. Point-of-care blood glucose readings for the first ICU day are 185 mg/dL, 223 mg/dL, 218 mg/dL, and 234 mg/dL. The intensivist would like to initiate hyperglycemic control. Which recommendation is most appropriate for glycemic control?AInsulin regular continuous infusion intravenously to a goal blood glucose 140 to 180 mg/dL.
choices: Insulin regular continuous infusion intravenously to a goal blood glucose 140 to 180 mg/dL
Insulin aspart moderate-coverage sliding scale subcutaneously to a goal blood glucose less than 180 mg/dL
Insulin glargine 10 units subcutaneously once daily at bedtime to a goal blood glucose less than 200 mg/dL
Insulin aspart 5 units subcutaneously three times daily plus insulin glargine 20 units subcutaneously once daily to a goal blood glucose less than 110 mg/dL
exp: The patient's blood glucose has continued to trend above 180 mg/dL, and the 2021 Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines recommend insulin continuous infusion in these cases for more immediate glycemic control. The target blood glucose for ICU patients is 140 to 180 mg/dL, supported by both the 2021 Surviving Sepsis Campaign and American Diabetes Association guidelines (Answer A is correct). Glycemic control will not predictably be achieved with just sliding scale insulin coverage when the blood glucose values are repeatedly greater than 180 mg/dL (Answer B is incorrect). Glycemic control will no

stem: Which patient would be best to recommend to receive phenobarbital as the first-line agent?A 2-year-old child experiencing absence seizures.B 6-month-old infant having infantile spasms.C3-week-old neonate in status epilepticus.D 6-year-old child after a first-time simple febrile seizure.
choices: 2-year-old child experiencing absence seizures
6-month-old infant having infantile spasms
3-week-old neonate in status epilepticus
6-year-old child after a first-time simple febrile seizure
exp: Similar to older patients, the drugs of choice for seizure management in children are based on seizure type. For neonatal seizures, phenobarbital remains a first-line agent, and alternative agents include levetiracetam and phenytoin (Answer C is correct). Ethosuximide is the drug of choice for absence seizures in children, and valproic acid is a potential alternative agent, although valproate is not recommended for use in children younger than 2 years because of increased risk of hepatotoxicity (Answer A is incorrect). Adrenocorticotropic hormone injectable gel is the drug of choice for infant

stem: A 38-year-old patient presents to the hospital with pain and swelling in the left lower extremity. Physical examination reveals diffuse erythema and swelling appearing to originate at the left ankle. There is no obvious purulent drainage, but some edema is present. The patient reports difficulty breathing when taking amoxicillin/clavulanate about 5 years ago. Which is the best empiric treatment for this patient?
choices: Aztreonam 1 g intravenously every 8 hours
Cefazolin 2 g intravenously every 8 hours
Ciprofloxacin 400 mg intravenously every 12 hours
Vancomycin 1000 mg intravenously every 12 hours
exp: This patient likely has cellulitis attributed to Streptococcus, given the lack of purulence and poorly demarcated presentation. Aztreonam lacks activity against gram-positive organisms, making it a poor empiric choice for most cases of cellulitis (Answer A is incorrect). The unique side chain of cefazolin allows it to be safely used when patients have a reported penicillin allergy (Answer B is correct). Ciprofloxacin does not have reliable activity against gram-positive organisms, even when it is reported as susceptible in vitro (Answer C is incorrect). Vancomycin would be appropriate empirica

stem: A 42-year-old patient reports headaches occurring 5 or 6 times per month that interfere with her ability to work. The episodes last 6 to 10 hours and are described as unilateral head pain, 8/10 on a 10-point scale. Her medical history includes epilepsy, peripheral vascular disease, and type 2 diabetes. Current medications are carbamazepine 200 mg orally twice daily, cilostazol 100 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg orally daily, and insulin glargine 26 units subcutaneously every evening. Which is the best acute treatment for this patient's headache disorder?
choices: Ubrogepant
Sumatriptan
Lasmiditan
Dihydroergotamine
exp: The patient is presenting with migraine headache (severe, given the pain rating 8/10). Triptans and dihydroergotamine should be avoided because of the patient's history of peripheral vascular disease (Answers B and D are incorrect). Ubrogepant should be avoided because of a drug interaction with carbamazepine (avoid use with strong CYP3A4 inducers; Answer A is incorrect). Lasmiditan is the best option for treating this patient's acute migraine headaches (Answer C is correct).

stem: A 72-year-old woman with a history of chronic hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents to your clinic for a routine follow-up. She currently takes lisinopril 20 mg daily and atorvastatin 40 mg daily. During the visit, she reports occasional forgetfulness with her medications but denies intentional nonadherence. You decide to assess her medication adherence using the Morisky Medication Adherence Scale (MMAS-8). Given her MMAS-8 score of 5, which is the most appropriate next step to improve her medication adherence?
choices: Prescribe a fixed-dose combination therapy to reduce pill burden
Offer a 30-minute counseling session to discuss the importance of medication adherence and the consequences of nonadherence
Suggest a pillbox and set up daily reminders on her telephone to enhance her medication-taking routine
Review the patient's medication regimen for potential side effects that may be contributing to her nonadherence
exp: The most appropriate next step to improve this patient's medication adherence is to suggest a pillbox and set up daily reminders on her telephone to enhance her medication-taking routine. The patient's MMAS-8 score of 5 suggests that forgetfulness is a significant barrier to adherence, making practical tools such as a pillbox and medication reminders essential for improving consistency (Answer C is correct). Although prescribing a fixed-dose combination therapy might reduce pill burden, it would not address the root cause of her nonadherence, which is likely forgetfulness (Answer A is incorrec

stem: A meta-analysis compared P2Y12 monotherapy with aspirin monotherapy on the primary end point (cardiovascular [CV] death, myocardial infarction [MI], or stroke) and secondary end point (major bleeding) in patients with coronary artery disease. For the primary end point, P2Y12 monotherapy compared with aspirin monotherapy resulted in a hazard ratio of 0.88 (95% CI, 0.79-0.97). For major bleeding, P2Y12 monotherapy compared with aspirin monotherapy resulted in a hazard ratio of 0.87 (95% CI, 0.70-1.09). Which most appropriately describes the results and statistical findings of this study?ACompared with aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors decreased the risk of CV death, MI, or stroke but did not affect major bleeding.
choices: P2Y12 monotherapy is superior to aspirin monotherapy on the primary endpoint with no difference in major bleeding
Compared with aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors increased the risk of CV death, MI, or stroke but did not affect major bleeding
Compared with aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors decreased the risk of CV death, MI, or stroke and reduced the risk of major bleeding
Compared with aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors did not affect the risk of CV death, MI, or stroke and did not affect the risk of major bleeding
exp: A hazard ratio of less than 1, when comparing aspirin with P2Y12 inhibitors, indicates that P2Y12 inhibitors reduce the risk of the event being investigated, either primary or secondary end point. A hazard ratio of greater than 1, when comparing aspirin with P2Y12 inhibitors, indicates that P2Y12 inhibitors increase the risk of the event being investigated, either primary or secondary end point. Regarding statistical significance, which is being assessed here, if the CI includes 1, the finding is not statistically significant. In this question, P2Y12 inhibitors reduced the risk of CV death, MI

stem: C.S. is a 66-year-old woman (height 163 cm, weight 75 kg) with newly diagnosed nonvalvular atrial fibrillation. Her current rate control includes bisoprolol 5 mg orally daily, and she is asymptomatic. Her medical history is otherwise significant for type 2 diabetes, diastolic heart failure (ejection fraction [EF] 55%), and osteoarthritis of the hip. Her medication regimen also consists of spironolactone 25 mg orally daily, metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg orally daily, dulaglutide 3 mg subcutaneously weekly, and meloxicam 15 mg orally daily. Her pulse rate is 72 beats/min and blood pressure is 126/80 mm Hg. Her SCr is 1.0 mg/dL with a CrCl of 55 mL/min. All other laboratory values are within normal limits. She denies use of tobacco or alcohol. Which best describes C.S.'s risk of stroke and major bleeding?
choices: High risk of stroke with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 2 and moderate risk of major bleed with HAS-BLED score of 2
High risk of stroke with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 3 and high risk of major bleed with HAS-BLED score of 3
High risk of stroke with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 4 and moderate risk of major bleed with HAS-BLED score of 2
High risk of stroke with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 3 and high risk of major bleed with HAS-BLED score of 2
exp: Given the patient's calculated CHA2DS2-VASc score of 4 (age 65-74 years, type 2 diabetes, heart failure, and female sex) and calculated HAS-BLED score of 2 (age older than 65 and medication use predisposing the patient to bleeding with meloxicam use), she is considered at high risk of stroke and moderate risk of major bleeding (Answer C is correct; Answers A, B, and D are incorrect). stem: A study designed to evaluate a threshold of NSAID use with respect to peptic ulcer disease (PUD) risk exists among patients with newly diagnosed PUD. Patients currently being treated for PUD were recruited to complete a survey regarding their NSAID use in the 6 months preceding their PUD diagnosis. The participants were asked to quantify how often they used NSAIDs and at what doses. Given this study design, which best depicts the type of bias that may result in inaccurate study results?ARecall.
choices: Recall
Publication
Channeling
Selection
exp: This retrospective study asks patients with newly diagnosed PUD to recall how frequently and at what doses they used NSAIDs in the 6 months preceding diagnosis. Because patients who already have the disease tend to search their memory for possible exposures more thoroughly (and differently) than unaffected people, self-reported exposure data collected after the outcome is prone to recall bias, which can distort the estimated NSAID–PUD association (Answer A is correct). Recall bias may be mitigated by using objective exposure measures (e.g., pharmacy dispensing records) rather than patient recall. Publication bias reflects selective publication of positive studies, channeling bias occurs when prognosis or characteristics influence which treatment a patient receives, and selection bias arises from how participants are enrolled or retained (Answers B, C, and D are incorrect). This study design is subject to recall bias because the outcomes rely on patients' retrospective assessment of their symptoms (Answer A is correct). Recall bias may be mitigated by selecting objective measures for outcomes of interest and assigning participants to blinded treatment, when possible. Public

stem: A 53-year-old man presents to the emergency department with cough, malaise, shortness of breath, and bone pain. Based on computed tomography scans, he is originally diagnosed with lung cancer. However, following a diagnostic bronchoscopy, it is found that he is infected with Blastomyces dermatitidis. Which is the best treatment for this patient?AItraconazole 200 mg orally twice daily for 24 weeks.
choices: Itraconazole 200 mg orally twice daily for 24 weeks
Fluconazole 400 mg orally daily for 2 weeks
Voriconazole 200 mg orally twice daily for 12 weeks
Micafungin 100mg intravenously daily for 2 weeks
exp: The recommended therapy for blastomycocsis is itraconazole for 24 weeks (Answer A is correct). Fluconazole and voriconazole might not be effective, and the treatment durations are too short (Answers B and C incorrect). Micafungin might have activity against Blastomyces dermatitidis, but clinical experience with the echinocandins is limited. If this was a severe infection needing intravenous treatment, amphotericin B is recommended (Answer D is incorrect).

stem: A 49-year-old man experienced a witnessed in-hospital cardiac arrest with pulseless ventricular tachycardia and achieved return of spontaneous circulation after 10 minutes. The physician selected a 37 degreesC target for targeted temperature management, and the patient will be cooled to target over 24 hours. The patient is mechanically ventilated, and current medications include fentanyl 50 mcg/hour continuous infusion, propofol 15 mcg/kg/minute, vancomycin 1 gram intravenously every 12 hours, piperacillin/tazobactam 3.375 grams intravenously every 8 hours, and heparin 5000 units subcutaneously every 8 hours. Which strategy is best to optimize the patient's clinical outcomes during targeted temperature management?
choices: Add rocuronium 1 mg/kg boluses as needed for shivering
Change heparin from prophylactic to therapeutic dosing
Add acetaminophen 650 mg orally every 4 hours as needed for fevers
Change propofol to midazolam infusion
exp: Fever prevention is an essential component of managing patients under targeted temperature management. As-needed acetaminophen is part of institutional anti-shivering protocols for its central mechanism of fever reduction (Answer C is correct). Although shivering prevention is needed to limit development of fevers during hypothermia, paralytic use is not routinely recommended because of safety considerations. Additionally, other medications are likely to achieve shivering prevention in most patients (Answer A is incorrect). Patients actually become more coagulopathic during hypothermia (caused

stem: A 22-year-old woman who uses heroin regularly has just discovered that she is pregnant. She has no other significant medical history. She wants to stop using heroin. Which medication would be best?
choices: Acamprosate
Chlordiazepoxide
Methadone
Naltrexone
category: 3B1. Research Methods (e.g., hypothesis, outcomes, study design)
exp: Methadone or buprenorphine is currently considered the standard of care for women who are pregnant who wish to detoxify from opioids (Answer C is correct). There is little research to support the use of naltrexone in women who are pregnant (Answer D is incorrect). Acamprosate is used for patients who have alcohol use disorder, not opioid use disorder (Answer A is incorrect). Chlordiazepoxide is used to treat acute withdrawal from alcohol, but it should not be combined with opioids and should be avoided in women who are pregnant (Answer B is incorrect).

stem: A woman with a history of persistent asthma presents to the ED because of worsening shortness of breath at rest. She is able to converse with some difficulty. She reports little improvement since she administered 4 puffs of her albuterol metered dose inhaler (MDI) at home 4 hours ago. She has no recent history of oral corticosteroid (OCS) use. Her forced expiratory volume in the first second of expiration (FEV1) is 48% of predicted. Her pulse rate is 102 beats/min. Her oxygen saturation on room air is 90%, and she is administered supplemental oxygen. Which therapy is best to initially treat this patient's asthma exacerbation?
choices: Albuterol and ipratropium by nebulizer every 20 minutes for 1 hour plus intravenous corticosteroids
Albuterol and ipratropium by nebulizer every 20 minutes for 1 hour plus OCS
Albuterol MDI up to 3 doses in the first hour
Albuterol MDI up to 3 doses in the first hour plus OCS
exp: The patient's clinical presentation is indicative of a severe asthma exacerbation, based on an FEV1 less than 50% of predicted with dyspnea at rest. She should receive an OCS in addition to albuterol and ipratropium combination by nebulizer every 20 minutes for 1 hour or continuously because this regimen is preferred to albuterol alone in severe asthma exacerbations treated in the ED (Answer B is correct). The regimens of either albuterol MDI alone or albuterol MDI plus OCS do not include the combination of albuterol and ipratropium by nebulizer, which is recommended in severe exacerbations (A

stem: A pharmacist at a community hospital has been charged with improving the reversal of anticoagulant medications and management of bleeding events, in accordance with the National Patient Safety Goals (NPSG). Which activity would be the best initial step to meet these goals?
choices: Conduct a medication use evaluation of current reversal practices
Submit a formulary request for new anticoagulant reversal agents
Develop and deliver an in-service presentation to physicians and nurses
Develop a standardized protocol based on current clinical practice guidelines and primary literature
exp: NPSG.03.05.01 pertains to the reduction of harm with the use of anticoagulant therapy. Current elements of performance include the use of approved protocols and evidence-based practice guidelines for the reversal of anticoagulation, management of bleeding events, and perioperative management of oral anticoagulation. Although conducting a medication use evaluation might help better ascertain current practices, it would not create an evidence-based method of treatment (Answer A is incorrect). Formulary management would be an important step after developing and approving evidence-based protocols

stem: A 32-year-old patient (BMI 29.7 kg/m2) with treatment-resistant depression and anxiety takes brexpiprazole, buspirone, levothyroxine, and metformin. Their depression was suboptimally treated with sertraline, so 2 weeks ago, it was changed to fluoxetine. Their partner brings them to the clinic because the patient is somnolent and unable to drive. The patient has been feeling progressively sleepier over the past week. Which agent is most likely responsible for the current clinical picture?ABrexpiprazole.
choices: Brexpiprazole
Buspirone
Levothyroxine
Metformin
exp: Brexpiprazole is a substrate of CYP2D6. When administered with strong CYP2D6 inhibitors, such as fluoxetine, the brexpiprazole dose should be halved. Thus, a dose that might have been compatible with sertraline may be too much with fluoxetine (Answer A is correct). Buspirone does not have a CYP-mediated interaction with fluoxetine (Answer B is incorrect). Although decreased (or absent) levothyroxine concentrations might lead to sedation, it does not interact with fluoxetine (Answer C is incorrect). Metformin is renally eliminated and does not interact with fluoxetine (Answer D is incorrect).

stem: J.P. has a history of primary severe mitral regurgitation. J.P. is preparing to undergo valve replacement with a mechanical valve. J.P.'s medical history is significant for obesity and a provoked deep vein thrombosis (DVT). Current medications include liraglutide 3 mg subcutaneously daily. J.P. does not use tobacco or alcohol products. Vitals include a heart rate of 84 beats/minute and blood pressure is 120/74 mm Hg. J.P.'s SCr is 1.0 mg/dL, correlating with a CrCl of 120 mL/minute. All other laboratory values and diagnostics are within normal limits. Which is the optimal anticoagulant regimen for preventing thrombosis in J.P. after mechanical mitral valve replacement?
choices: Dose-adjust warfarin to INR goal of 2.0-3.0 (INR target 2.5)
Dose-adjust warfarin to INR goal of 2.5-3.5 (INR target 3.0)
Dose-adjust warfarin to INR goal of 2.0-3.0 for first 3 months, then target 1.5-2.0 with aspirin 81 mg orally daily
Dose-adjust warfarin to INR goal of 2.5-3.5 for first 3 months, then target 2.0-2.5 with aspirin 81 mg orally daily
exp: Given the patient's receipt of a mechanical valve in the mitral position regardless of the presence/absence of a thromboembolic risk factor (e.g., history of DVT), standard-intensity therapy to an INR target of 3.0 is indicated (Answer B is correct). Answer A would be correct if the patient were receiving a mechanical valve replacement in the aortic position and no thromboembolism risk factors were present (Answer A is incorrect). A low-intensity vitamin K antagonist with concomitant low-dose aspirin is not recommended in the setting of the patient's current risk factors and valve type (Answer

stem: A 64-year-old patient has a long-standing history of uncontrolled diabetes and receives hemodialysis three times weekly. Current labs include: hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL, hematocrit 27%, mean corpuscular volume 88 fL, iron 85 mcg/dL, total iron binding capacity 270 mcg/dL, TSat 31%, and serum ferritin 587 ng/mL. Which is most appropriate to initiate for this patient?
choices: Oral ferrous sulfate
Intravenous sodium ferric gluconate complex
Intravenous darbepoetin alfa
No therapy, repeat labs in 3 months
exp: This patient with G5 chronic kidney disease has a hemoglobin less than 13 g/dL, indicating an anemia workup is appropriate. Iron indices are at Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes goals of transferrin saturation greater than 30%, at 31%, and serum ferritin greater than 500 ng/mL, at 587 ng/mL (Answers A and B are incorrect). Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents should be initiated when hemoglobin falls below 10 g/dL and iron indices are at goal (Answer D is incorrect; Answer C is correct).

stem: A 71-year-old woman has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. She smokes half a pack of cigarettes per day and is lactose intolerant with minimal intake of dairy products. She takes calcium 1200 mg orally per day in divided doses and vitamin D 600 international units/day orally. Her calculated creatinine clearance (CrCl) is 60-70 mL/minute. Her bone mineral density T-score is −2.6 at the hip and −2.1 at the spine. Her Fracture Risk Assessment Tool score indicates she has a 10-year probability of a major osteoporotic fracture of 22% and a 10-year probability of a hip fracture of 11%. Of the following, which is the best therapy to recommend?
choices: Continue calcium 1200 mg/vitamin D 600 international units orally daily
Add abaloparatide 80 mcg subcutaneously daily and continue calcium 1200 mg/vitamin D 600 international units orally daily
Add romosozumab 210 mg subcutaneously every month, continue calcium 1200 mg orally daily, and increase to vitamin D 800 international units orally daily
Add risedronate 35 mg orally every week, continue calcium 1200 mg orally daily, and increase to vitamin D 800 international units orally daily
exp: Drug therapy for osteoporosis is indicated for the following: a hip or vertebral fracture, a bone mineral density (BMD) T-score below −2.5 at the femoral neck or spine, or a BMD T-score between −1.0 and −2.5 at the femoral neck or spine and a 10-year probably of hip fracture 3% or greater or a 10-year probably of a major osteoporosis-related fracture of 20% or greater based on the Fracture Risk Assessment Tool (FRAX) system. This patient needs therapy because her T-score is less than −2.5 at the hip, and her 10-year probability of hip fracture is at least 3% or her 10-year probability of major

stem: A patient recently purchased compounded semaglutide online because of supply shortages at his local pharmacy. The medication arrived at his house with 2 medication vials requiring reconstitution, diluent, and four 100-unit insulin syringes. However, the patient was unfamiliar with measuring medications with an insulin syringe and did not realize that the vial was designed for multiple doses. The patient administered a full 100 units of medication, or 1 mL, instead of the correct dose of 50 units or 0.5 mL. The patient experienced severe nausea and vomiting for several days after giving the injection and went to urgent care for intravenous fluids and ondansetron. Which best depicts the National Coordinating Council for Medication Error Reporting and Prevention (NCC MERP) category in which this medication error is classified? NCC MERP Category
choices: Category CB Category DCCategory ED Category F
An error occurred but the error did not reach the patient
An error occurred that reached the patient but did not cause patient harm

An error occurred that reached the patient and required monitoring to confirm that it resulted in no harm to the patient and/or required intervention to preclude harm E An error occurred that may have contributed to or resulted in temporary harm to the patient and required intervention F An error occurred that may have contributed to or resulted in temporary harm to the patient and required initial or prolonged hospitalization G An error occurred that may have contributed to or resulted in permanent patient harm H An error occurred that required intervention necessary to sustain life I An error occurred that may have contributed to or resulted in the patient's death
exp: Answer C is correct. Answer A is incorrect because the error reached the patient and did cause patient harm. Answer D is incorrect because the patient did not require initial or prolonged hospitalization. Answer C is a better answer than Answer B because the patient did require intervention (intravenous fluids and ondansetron) and did not visit a health care professional to preclude harm.

stem: A clinical pharmacist is evaluating the cost-effectiveness of 2 therapies for a patient with chronic hypertension: drug A, a new brand name medication; and drug B, a generic alternative. Drug A has shown superior efficacy in clinical trials, but it is significantly more expensive than drug B. The pharmacist is tasked with determining the most cost-effective option according to the available pharmacoeconomic data. Which pharmacoeconomic analysis is most appropriate for evaluating which drug provides the best value for the cost?ACost-utility analysis (CUA), which compares the cost of the 2 drugs relative to the quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) they provide.
choices: Cost-utility analysis (CUA), which compares the cost of the 2 drugs relative to the quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) they provide
Cost-minimization analysis (CMA), which assumes that the 2 drugs are equally effective and compares only their costs
Cost-benefit analysis (CBA), which compares the costs of the 2 drugs with their monetary benefits
Budget impact analysis (BIA), which compares the financial impact of the drugs on the hospital's budget
exp: Answer A is the best answer in this scenario because it allows for a comparison of both the costs and health outcomes (eg, QALYs). Because drug A has superior efficacy but higher cost, CUA would allow for the comparison of both its increased effectiveness and its higher costs relative to the QALYs gained. CMA is not appropriate because it assumes that the 2 drugs have equivalent outcomes, and the analysis focuses solely on the cost comparison (Answer B is incorrect). Answer C is incorrect; although a CBA compares the costs of the drugs with their monetary benefits, it does not directly account

stem: A 72-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with chronic cough and dyspnea for the past 4 months. She denies dyspnea at rest and recent exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) within the past year. Her COPD Assessment Test (CAT) score is 9. Which regimen is best to recommend as initial maintenance therapy for this patient's COPD?
choices: Fluticasone-salmeterol
Fluticasone furoate-umeclidinium-vilanterol
Tiotropium-olodaterol
Umeclidinium
exp: According to the Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease guidelines, this patient's COPD is classified as group A because she has not had any exacerbations within the past year and her CAT score is less than 10. A long-acting anticholinergic-muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) is preferred for group A (Answer D is correct). An inhaled corticosteroid (ICS)-long-acting β2-agonist (LABA) combination is not recommended in the treatment of COPD (Answer A is incorrect). A LAMA-LABA-ICS combination is considered an initial treatment only in group E if the patient has an eosinophil count of

stem: A 24-year-old woman with a history of tonic-clonic seizures achieves symptom control with phenytoin 100 mg by mouth three times daily. Her serum phenytoin concentration is normally 13 mg/L. She is adherent to therapy and was recently prescribed carbamazepine, with the most recent laboratory values from a clinic visit showing a total serum phenytoin concentration of 6.5 mg/L and a free concentration of albumin of 2.5 g/dL. Given the current information, which changes would be best to suggest?
choices: Increase the phenytoin dose to 200 mg by mouth three times daily
Increase the phenytoin dose to 100 mg by mouth four times daily
Decrease the phenytoin dose to 100 mg by mouth twice daily
Maintain the current dosage
category: 2B6. Patient and Caregiver Education and Counseling
exp: The patient has hypoalbuminemia. Phenytoin concentrations should be corrected for low albumin. The patient's corrected phenytoin serum concentration is approximately 10.8 mg/L (6.5 / [0.2 x 2.5 + 0.1]), at the low end of the therapeutic range (10-20 mg/L); because concurrent carbamazepine (an enzyme inducer) continues to lower phenytoin exposure, a modest dose increase is still warranted. The dosage should be increased (Answer B is correct). The dosage should be increased slowly because of phenytoin's nonlinear kinetics; doubling the dose may lead to excessive exposure and contribute to toxicity (Answer A is incorrect). Because the patient is not exhibiting seizures, aggressive dose titrations are not essential. Decreasing or maintaining the current dose is not

stem: Which best depicts the organization that is tasked with maintaining and distributing, when necessary, medications from the Strategic National Stockpile program?
choices: Trust for America's Health
State health departments
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
State pharmacy organizations
exp: The CDC is responsible for developing guidelines and resources to aid in disaster relief, including management of the Strategic National Stockpile (Answer C is correct). Emergency preparedness professionals at state and local health departments perform hazard vulnerability analyses and help implement plans in an emergency situation specific for the area of their responsibility, which may include distribution of medications. However, they do not help maintain a stockpile (Answer B is incorrect). Trust for America's Health evaluates 10 indicators of emergency preparedness to follow overall tre

stem: A.B., an 82-year-old man, presents to the geriatrics office accompanied by his daughter, who is concerned about her father's increased "forgetfulness," inability to dress himself in the morning, and frequent coughing after eating. The patient's medical history is significant for hypertension, coronary artery disease, heart failure, and Parkinson disease (PD). He takes the following medications: carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 mg orally three times daily, lisinopril 20 mg orally daily, furosemide 20 mg orally daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq orally daily, and carvedilol 12.5 mg orally twice daily. His Mini-Mental State Examination score is 10/30. Blood tests and urinalysis were unremarkable. No nutritional deficiencies are noted. Current blood pressure is 114/72 mm Hg and pulse rate is 64 beats/min. The patient is diagnosed with dementia of advanced PD. Which therapeutic recommendation is best to initiate for A.B. at this time?
choices: Rivastigmine 4.6-mg transdermal patch daily
Pimavanserin 34 mg daily
Donepezil 10 mg daily
Memantine 10 mg daily
exp: Cholinesterase inhibitors (CIs) can be used in dementia of advanced PD, of which efficacy between agents does not significantly differ. The patient's coughing after eating may indicate dysphagia, which is common in advanced PD. Because of this, a transdermal CI like rivastigmine may be better tolerated (Answer A is correct). Currently, the patient has no signs attributable to PD psychosis, making pimavanserin inappropriate at this time (Answer B is incorrect). Although donepezil and memantine are only indicated for Alzheimer disease dementia, they have been used to treat various types of demen

stem: A pharmacist is counseling a 68-year-old patient who has just been prescribed a new medication for hypertension. During the counseling, the patient expresses concern regarding the side effects and would prefer not to take the medication because of a negative experience with a similar medication in the past. The pharmacist is understanding but believes that the medication prescribed is in the patient's best interest, given that the patient has more than 1 indication for the medication. Which best describes what the pharmacist should do in this situation?
choices: The pharmacist should respect the patient's wishes and suggest alternative treatments that do not require use of the medication, even if they are less beneficial
The pharmacist should provide the patient with information about the prescribed medication, including potential side effects, and recommend alternative therapies with the prescriber
The pharmacist should emphasize the risks of nonadherence and strongly urge the patient to take the drug as directed, disregarding the patient's personal history
The pharmacist should not fill the prescription and should contact the prescriber to let them know the patient has refused treatment
exp: The pharmacist should acknowledge the patient's concerns while providing objective, evidence-based information about the prescribed medication and not disregard the patient's concerns (Answer C is incorrect). Offering alternative therapies allows the patient to make an informed decision about their treatment (Answer B is correct). Although patient autonomy is important, completely avoiding a discussion of the prescribed medication's benefits might lead to suboptimal treatment (Answer A is incorrect). It is good that the pharmacist is going to contact the prescriber about not filling the medica

stem: A hospital is implementing a computerized physician order entry (CPOE) system to improve medication prescribing and reduce medication errors. The system allows physicians to enter medication orders directly into the electronic health record rather than using paper orders. Which best describes the primary benefit of using a CPOE system in medication prescribing?
choices: Eliminates the need for pharmacists to review and verify medication orders before dispensing
Provides real-time alerts for drug-drug interactions, allergies, and dosing errors, reducing the risk of medication errors
Standardizes medication orders, making it easier for clinicians to prescribe drugs without considering patient-specific factors
Reduces the need for clinician involvement in medication prescribing, leading to faster order processing
category: 3B1. Research Methods (e.g., hypothesis, outcomes, study design)
exp: The primary benefit of a CPOE system is its ability to provide real-time alerts for potential issues such as drug-drug interactions, allergies, and dosing errors, which significantly reduces the risk of medication errors and enhances patient safety (Answer B is correct). Although pharmacists still play an essential role in reviewing orders (Answer A is incorrect), a CPOE system is designed to improve prescribing by considering patient-specific factors (Answer C is incorrect) and supporting clinician decision-making rather than replacing it (Answer D is incorrect).

stem: Which organization would be the best resource for identifying clinical preventive services to be provided for patients that are evidence-based and not focused on immunizations?
choices: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA)
U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF)
Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality (AHRQ)
exp: USPSTF recommends health screenings that are evidence-based and that do not include immunizations (Answer C is correct). The CDC identifies immunization schedules on the basis of recommendations made by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (Answer A is incorrect). IDSA provides evidence-based resources for antimicrobial prescribing, which include both disease treatment and prevention (Answer B is incorrect). AHRQ does not provide recommendations for care but focuses on measuring quality of outcomes and ways to improve quality (Answer D is incorrect).

stem: L.P. is a 61-year-old man presenting to the emergency department for symptoms of right-sided paralysis and dysphasia. His wife reports these symptoms starting 5 hours ago, and they have not improved. L.P. is diagnosed with a major ischemic stroke. He has hypertension, hyperthyroidism, type 2 diabetes, and opioid use disorder. Electrocardiogram on admission shows normal sinus rhythm. Which treatment recommendation is best for L.P.?
choices: Alteplase
Aspirin and clopidogrel
Warfarin
Apixaban
exp: Warfarin is only indicated for stroke prevention with atrial fibrillation, not acute stroke treatment (Answer C is incorrect). Apixaban is indicated for secondary stroke prophylaxis in patients with atrial fibrillation; this patient does not have atrial fibrillation thus apixaban is not indicated (Answer D is incorrect). The patient's electrocardiogram shows normal sinus rhythm on admission, which helps rule out atrial fibrillation. Dual antiplatelet therapy is indicated for secondary stroke prevention in patients with major or minor stroke, to be started within 24 hours of symptom onset. Answ

stem: A 41-year-old patient presents to the ED with a headache and severe neck pain. The patient reports that light and sound worsen the headache. A lumbar puncture is performed, and the CSF culture grows Streptococcus pneumoniae. Which prophylactic strategy is most recommended for household contacts?
choices: No prophylaxis is necessary for household adults or children; monitor for signs and symptoms consistent with meningitis
Adults should receive rifampin 600 mg orally daily; children should receive 20 mg/kg orally daily for 4 days
Adults should receive rifampin 600 mg orally twice daily; children should receive 10 mg/kg orally twice daily for 4 doses
Adults should receive the 20-valent pneumococcal vaccine; children should receive the 15-valent pneumococcal vaccine
exp: No prophylaxis is recommended for people exposed to a person with confirmed pneumococcal meningitis (Answer A is correct). Pneumococcal vaccines can be administered to close contacts if they meet CDC vaccine criteria, but vaccines do not offer acute protection for invasive pneumococcal disease (Answer D is incorrect). The daily dosing of rifampin is recommended for people exposed to Haemophilus influenzae meningitis, and twice-daily dosing is recommended for those exposed to Neisseria meningitidis meningitis (Answers B and C are incorrect).

stem: Which patient is best to recommend for treatment with tolvaptan according to the presenting disease and sodium laboratory value?A64-year-old patient with hypervolemia and congestive heart failure; Na 126 mEq/L.B 72-year-old patient taking chronic chlorthalidone; Na 128 mEq/L.C 65-year-old patient with severe diarrhea and vomiting; Na 129 mEq/L.D 43-year-old patient with cerebral salt wasting from glioblastoma; Na 130 mEq/L.
choices: 64-year-old patient with hypervolemia and congestive heart failure; Na 126 mEq/L
72-year-old patient taking chronic chlorthalidone; Na 128 mEq/L
65-year-old patient with severe diarrhea and vomiting; Na 129 mEq/L
43-year-old patient with cerebral salt wasting from glioblastoma; Na 130 mEq/L
exp: Tolvaptan should only be used in hypervolemic or euvolemic hyponatremia. Cerebral salt wasting is a type of hypovolemic hyponatremia (Answer D is incorrect). Chronic use of a thiazide diuretic and the symptoms of severe diarrhea and vomiting can also both cause hypovolemic hyponatremia, so tolvaptan would not be appropriate (Answers B and C are incorrect). The most appropriate use of tolvaptan is for a patient with hypervolemia and congestive heart failure (Answer A is correct).

stem: A 45-year-old woman with hypertension presents for a primary care follow-up. Her current medications include chlorthalidone 25 mg daily. Which is the most appropriate choice of vaccine(s) to recommend during the patient's visit taking place in October?
choices: IIV
IIV and recombinant zoster vaccine (RZV)
20-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine and Tdap
IIV and Tdap
exp: All individuals are recommended to receive an annual influenza vaccine. In addition, this patient is due for a Tdap booster, which is recommended every 10 years. Because the patient's last Tdap was 11 years ago (>10 years), either a Td or Tdap booster is appropriate in this situation (Answer D is correct). Answer A is incorrect because it does not include Td or Tdap. The patient is not a candidate for the RZV because of age younger than 50 and lack of immunocompromising conditions (Answer B is incorrect). Answer C is incorrect because it does not include IIV, and the pa

stem: A 19-year-old male patient with type 1 diabetes is admitted to the hospital for diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) occurring after contracting an influenza A infection. The patient has an insulin pump and stated that he kept administering his usual dose despite not eating as much when getting sick. Then, when he realized his glucose concentrations were low, he stopped his pump altogether, which resulted in DKA. The patient is a college freshman this year and is living alone for the first time. Previously, the patient's mother helped with his type 1 diabetes management. Which would be best to educate the patient on to help prevent his readmission for DKA?ASick day insulin management.
choices: Sick day insulin management
Consistent carbohydrate diet
Insulin pump technique
Importance of regular eye examinations
exp: The patient has poor insight regarding how to manage his type 1 diabetes in the setting of illness. To prevent readmissions for DKA, counseling on sick day insulin management is warranted (Answer A is correct). Counseling on a consistent carbohydrate diet is important for general diabetes management but is not relevant to this patient's reason for admission in the setting of acute illness (Answer B is incorrect).
